title: Today's Markets Brief: U.S. confirms second Texas screwworm case, Canada restricts livestock imports
summary: A look at the U.S. screwworm case and its market impact, plus robo-advisers' AI limitations.
imageAlt: Image from source article for U.S. confirms second Texas screwworm case, Canada restricts livestock imports
imageCaption: U.S. confirms second Texas screwworm case, Canada restricts livestock imports
imageSourceTitle: U.S. confirms second Texas screwworm case, Canada restricts livestock imports

body.text: I'm Leo Stone, and this is your markets brief. We're keeping an eye on a couple of interesting developments. First, the U.S. has confirmed a second case of screwworm in Texas. This parasitic infestation affects livestock, and Canada has responded by restricting imports from certain U.S. regions. It's a story that's got everyone from farmers to investors paying attention.

The screwworm larvae feed on the living tissue of warm-blooded animals, creating severe wounds that can be fatal if left untreated. This is a significant concern for farmers and the livestock industry, not just in Texas but also in neighboring regions. The Canadian restrictions are likely aimed at protecting their own agricultural sector, and it's a reminder of how quickly these kinds of issues can have far-reaching impacts.

In other news, it seems that most robo-advisers won't be profiting from Wall Street's AI-generated stock picks anytime soon. While these platforms excel at tasks like tax-loss harvesting and portfolio discipline, generating market-beating returns isn't their strong suit. It's a bit of a reality check for those of us who thought AI would revolutionize investing overnight.
